question: Computer Graphics mainly deal with:
option_c: Creation and manipulation of visual content
description: Computer Graphics is fundamentally about the creation and manipulation of visual content using computers — from images and animations to interactive graphics.

question: Computer Graphics simplify the process of displaying:
description: CG simplifies the process of displaying pictures of any size on a computer screen, using various algorithms and techniques.

question: Computer Graphics are used in all of the following EXCEPT:
description: CG is used in entertainment, education, scientific visualization, and many more fields — but database normalization is a database design process unrelated to graphics.

question: Which of the following is NOT a type of computer graphics?
description: The main types are Raster, Vector, 3D, Interactive, and Real-Time. There is no category called Binary graphics.

question: Raster graphics are made up of:
description: Raster (bitmap) graphics are made up of tiny dots called pixels (picture elements). Vector graphics, by contrast, use mathematical equations.

question: True or False: Computer graphics deal only with 2D images.
description: False. CG deals with both 2D and 3D images — modern CG heavily focuses on 3D modelling and rendering.

question: True or False: Raster graphics are composed of pixels.
description: True. Raster (bitmap) graphics are composed of pixels — tiny dots that represent visual information.

question: True or False: Vector graphics lose quality when scaled.
description: False. Vector graphics keep their quality at any scale because they use mathematical equations rather than fixed pixels. This is opposite to raster graphics.

question: True or False: Computer graphics are used in virtual reality.
description: True. Virtual reality is a major application area of CG — VR relies on real-time 3D rendering for immersive experiences.

question: True or False: Animation is a single static image.
description: False. Animation is a sequence of frames where each frame is slightly different — creating the illusion of motion. A single static image is just an image.

question: True or False: 3D rendering produces a 3D image from a 2D scene.
option_b: False — it produces a 2D image from a 3D scene
description: False. 3D rendering is the opposite — it generates a 2D image from a digital 3D scene, projecting the 3D world onto a 2D screen.

question: True or False: Z-buffer is related to depth storage.
description: True. The Z-buffer is a 2D data system used for depth calculation and storage — it determines which objects are in front of others.

question: True or False: Texture mapping adds surface details to objects.
description: True. Texture / Bump Mapping displays color, material, and object details — adding realistic surface appearance to 3D models.

question: True or False: Ray tracing creates realistic lighting effects.
description: True. Ray Tracing (and Ray Casting) are useful rendering techniques for creating natural lighting effects — including reflections, refractions, and shadows.

question: True or False: Raster images are ideal for logos.
option_b: False — vector graphics are ideal for logos
description: False. Vector graphics are ideal for logos because they scale without losing quality. Raster images are better for detailed photographs.

question: Computer graphics is mainly considered as:
option_b: An art of drawing pictures using computers
description: Computer graphics is the art of drawing pictures on computer screens with the help of programming, involving computations, creation, and manipulation of data.

question: Computer graphics involve all of the following EXCEPT:
description: CG involves computations, creation, and manipulation of data — but network routing is a networking concern, not a CG concern.

question: The primary output device in a graphical system is:
description: The primary output device is the video monitor. Keyboard and mouse are input devices; the printer is a secondary output device.

question: The main component of a video monitor is:
description: In the classic CRT monitor model, the Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) is the main element. (Modern monitors use LCD/LED, but the lecture refers to CRT as the canonical example.)

question: Which component emits electrons in a CRT?
description: The electron gun emits the beam of electrons (cathode rays). The deflection system directs them, and the phosphor screen lights up where they hit.

question: True or False: Phosphor emits light when struck by electrons.
description: True. When the electron beam hits the screen, the phosphor coating emits a small spot of light at each contacted position — this is how CRTs render images.

question: True or False: Raster scan displays images line by line.
description: True. In a raster scan, the electron beam is swept across the screen one row at a time, from top to bottom.

question: True or False: The frame buffer (refresh buffer) stores intensity values of pixels.
description: True. The refresh buffer / frame buffer holds the set of intensity values for all the screen points; values are retrieved and painted on the screen one row at a time.

question: True or False: Pixel and pel mean the same thing.
option_c: Pel is for vector, pixel for raster
option_d: Pel is a deprecated term only
description: True. Each screen point is referred to as a pixel (picture element) or pel — both terms mean the same thing.

question: True or False: Random scan is also known as vector scan.
description: True. Random Scan (where the electron beam is directed only to parts of the screen where the picture is drawn) is also called vector display, stroke-writing display, or calligraphic display.

question: Which technique calculates the surface of computer graphics traditionally?
description: Scanline is a traditional rendering technique that calculates computer graphics surface.

question: Each pixel in a raster image consists of which subpixel components that mix to create colors?
description: Pixels consist of RGB (Red, Green, Blue) subpixels, which mix to create the diverse colors visible on a screen.

question: What does 'resolution' refer to in an image?
option_a: The size of the file in MB
option_b: How many pixels are in row and column manner
description: Resolution refers to how many pixels are arranged in rows and columns. More pixels = more detailed image.

question: What is the purpose of the deflection system in a CRT?
option_b: To direct the electron beam towards specified positions on the screen
description: The deflection system (along with the focusing system) directs the electron beam towards specified positions on the phosphor-coated screen.
